items.title: Make-to-stock full cycle from components to finished stock
items.description: A reusable scenario receives component stock, authors a BOM, creates a production order, issues materials, passes quality and completes, then asserts the finished good is on hand in the correct quantity. It proves the manufacturing lifecycle end-to-end against the real stock ledger.
items.applicability: Regression coverage and a worked template for the basic produce-to-stock flow.
items.example: Receive 100 components, BOM of 2 components per finished unit, order 10 -> after complete the warehouse holds 10 finished units.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive the scenario through the real services (manufacturing and stock) rather than mutating entities directly, so the test exercises the actual engine. Test implication: assert finished on-hand equals the produced quantity. Honest boundary: covers a single-level make-to-stock cycle only; multi-level assemblies are out of scope.

items.title: Quality fail then rework passes to completion
items.description: A scenario issues materials, records a failing quality inspection, confirms completion is blocked, then re-inspects with a pass and completes successfully. It proves the rework path: a fail is not terminal and a later pass legitimately permits completion.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for the quality gate and rework loop.
items.example: Issue -> inspect fail -> Complete throws -> inspect pass -> Complete succeeds and the order reaches Completed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: allow re-inspection from the failed state so rework is reachable without re-issuing materials. Test implication: assert completion throws after a fail and succeeds after a subsequent pass. Honest boundary: rework here is a re-inspection only; it does not model additional material consumption or scrap during rework.

items.title: Production cost flows into finished-good valuation
items.description: A scenario produces a finished good and then asserts the finished item's moving-average valuation rate equals the per-unit production cost derived from the materials consumed. It proves costing is not just recorded on the order but actually carried into stock valuation.
items.applicability: Regression coverage linking production costing to inventory valuation.
items.example: 20 components at 50 each (1000) over 10 produced units gives a finished-good valuation rate of 100.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: receive the finished good at the computed unit cost so the stock valuation rate reflects production. Test implication: assert the finished item's valuation rate equals material cost divided by produced quantity. Honest boundary: only material cost flows to valuation; labour and overhead are not part of the cost and so not part of the valuation.

items.title: Procure, produce, then sell a finished good
items.description: A scenario procures components into stock, produces finished units via the manufacturing lifecycle, then sells some of the finished goods through the sales flow, asserting both that remaining finished stock equals produced minus sold and that accounts receivable has increased. It chains procurement, manufacturing and selling in one cycle.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for the full procure-to-produce-to-sell value chain.
items.example: Produce 20 finished units, sell 5 -> 15 finished units remain on hand and accounts receivable is positive.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route each stage through its real service and respect the sales submission/approval path so the GL effects are genuine. Test implication: assert remaining finished stock and a positive receivable after the sale. Honest boundary: the chain stops at the receivable — settlement and delivery-note documents are not exercised here.

items.title: Sales reporting is consistent between register and summary
items.description: After a submitted and approved sale, a scenario asserts the sales register has the expected single row and that the customer summary's total reconciles to the register's grand total. It proves the reporting layer agrees with itself on the same submitted document set.
items.example: One sale -> SalesRegister has one row and SalesSummaryByCustomer totals equal the register's grand total.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive both reports from the same submitted set so they cannot diverge. Test implication: assert the register count and that summary total equals register total. Honest boundary: consistency is asserted at grand-total level, not line by line.

items.title: Receivables aging is current for a new invoice
items.description: After a fresh sale, a scenario asserts that the receivables aging total equals the amount sitting in the 0-30 bucket, proving that newly posted invoices age into the current bucket and that the buckets sum to the total.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for aging-bucket placement of recent invoices.
items.example: Sell today -> the entire outstanding receivable shows in the 0-30 bucket and aging total equals the 0-30 figure.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: age from the invoice posting date against an as-of date so a same-day invoice lands in the first bucket. Test implication: assert total equals the 0-30 bucket for fresh invoices. Honest boundary: only current-bucket placement is asserted; movement into older buckets over time is not simulated in this scenario.

items.title: Tax summary reconciles with sales
items.description: After a submitted sale, a scenario asserts that the tax summary's output tax equals the summed tax total of the sales register. This proves the tax position is derived from, and reconciles to, the same submitted sales documents.
items.example: Sell with tax -> TaxSummary.OutputTax equals the sum of TaxTotal across the sales register.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute output tax from submitted sales tax totals so the two figures share a source. Test implication: assert output tax equals register tax total. Honest boundary: reconciliation is to total output tax; per-rate or filing-period breakdowns are not asserted.

items.title: Stock valuation reconciles after a sale
items.description: After receiving and then selling part of an item's stock, a scenario asserts that stock valuation reports the remaining quantity. This proves the valuation report tracks the authoritative ledger through a sale movement.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for inventory valuation after outbound movement.
items.example: Receive 100, sell 20 -> stock valuation shows the item with quantity 80.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: read valuation from the stock ledger so a sale's MoveOut is reflected automatically. Test implication: assert valuation quantity equals received minus sold. Honest boundary: the scenario asserts remaining quantity; it does not separately re-derive the moving-average value formula.

items.title: Reorder alert fires for low stock
items.description: After a sale leaves an item below a chosen threshold, a scenario asserts the item appears in the reorder report run at that threshold. This proves the low-stock alert reacts to real outbound movement rather than a static seed value.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for threshold-based reorder alerting.
items.example: After selling down to 80 on hand, the item appears in a reorder report at threshold 90.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build the reorder report on the live valuation so it reflects post-sale quantities. Test implication: assert the depleted item is flagged at a threshold above its remaining quantity. Honest boundary: the threshold is supplied by the scenario; no per-item reorder level is modelled.

items.title: Work-order summary tracks status transitions
items.description: A scenario creates a production order and confirms it shows under Draft in the work-order summary, then runs the lifecycle to completion and confirms it appears under Completed. This proves the summary report reflects the live pipeline as orders advance.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for manufacturing pipeline reporting.
items.example: New order -> summary shows a Draft entry; after issue/pass/complete -> summary shows a Completed entry.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive the lifecycle through the manufacturing service so the summary reads genuine status changes. Test implication: assert the order is summarised under Draft, then under Completed. Honest boundary: the summary tracks status counts; it does not report timing or duration between transitions.

items.title: Purchase reporting reflects procurement
items.description: After procurement creates a submitted purchase invoice, a scenario asserts the purchase register is non-empty and that the supplier summary contains the seeded supplier. This proves procurement activity surfaces in the purchase-side reports.
items.example: Receive stock via a purchase -> PurchaseRegister is non-empty and PurchaseSummaryBySupplier contains that supplier.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: generate procurement through the real purchase flow so the register and summary populate from genuine documents. Test implication: assert the register is non-empty and the supplier appears in the summary. Honest boundary: asserts presence of procurement, not full three-way match against goods receipt and payment.

items.title: Combined business day keeps the GL balanced and reports populated
items.description: A scenario produces a finished good and then sells it within one cycle, and asserts that the general-ledger debit and credit totals are equal, the sales register is populated, and the work-order summary shows a completed order. It proves manufacturing and selling together leave the books balanced and the reports consistent.
items.applicability: Regression coverage for a mixed production-and-sale business day touching stock, the GL and reporting at once.
items.example: Produce 10, sell 4 finished units -> GL debit equals credit, the sales register has rows, and the work-order summary shows a Completed order.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run production and sale through their real services and read GL totals from the accounting service so the balance check is meaningful. Test implication: assert GL debit equals credit and both reports are populated after the combined flow. Honest boundary: the scenario proves the implemented engine end-to-end; HR/payroll, POS, e-commerce and full delivery-note or return document chains are not exercised here.
